extern type Number;
cap Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self }
extern fn js_add(a: Number, b: Number): Number;
impl Number: Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self = resume(js_add(a, b)) }

fn quadruple(x: Number): Number = Add.add(Add.add(x, x), Add.add(x, x))
fn main(): Number = quadruple(5)

===EXPECT===
# Two-level (nested) Perform chain: each `Add.add` gets its impl body inlined
# recursively. Three Performs of `Add.add` should each resolve to a direct
# `js_add(...)` call. With two call-sites inside `quadruple` plus the outer
# call from `main`, heuristic D (single non-trivial caller + multi-use body)
# clones the fn as `quadruple__lto` rather than inlining it at each call site.
# Key invariants:
#   - `quadruple__lto` exists (clone produced by LTO).
#   - Every `Add.add` Perform is inlined — `js_add(` appears at least 3 times
#     in the cloned body, and no `__caps.Add_Number` / `__impl_Number_Add.add`
#     property access leaks into it.
#   - `__main_cps` calls `quadruple__lto(...)` directly rather than threading
#     the cap bundle through `quadruple`.
# Known v1 follow-up (non-blocking): the outer `main()` wrapper still
# constructs `{ Add_Number: __impl_Number_Add(__identity) }` because main's
# `cap: Infer(...)` floor is preserved across the post-LTO re-typecheck.
# Assertions target `__main_cps` and `quadruple__lto`, not the outer wrapper.
quadruple__lto
js_add(x, __lto_b
__main_cps
quadruple__lto(5)
!__caps.Add_Number.add
!__impl_Number_Add.add(
